item.title: The King of the North, Andy Burnham, Won’t Save Britain
item.description: For the country, Andy Burnham looks a lot like more of the same.

item.title: Women Have Earned Our Menopause Revolution
item.description: A new generation is not willing to settle for being invisible.

item.title: The Left Needs to Reclaim Its Optimism
item.description: Can a more patriotic leftism help the Democrat Party rebrand? Bhaskar Sunkara, author of “The Socialist Manifesto,” talks on “Interesting Times” about how liberals have damaged the Democratic party’s brand by embracing a pessimistic narrative of American history.

item.title: An American Strike Killed Iranian Civilians. Where’s the Accountability?
item.description: The Trump administration has failed to publicly address the mistaken missile strike that killed children during the Iran war.

item.title: ‘A Million Men With Kalashnikovs Are Dangerous’
item.description: A Q&A with Kori Schake on the Russia-Ukraine war.

item.title: What Americans Need to Understand About China
item.description: Kevin Rudd, a former prime minister of Australia, went back to school to try to understand how the Chinese president thinks.

item.title: The Housing Crisis Is Also a Retirement Crisis
item.description: A home has stopped being just a place to live — it has also become an asset.

item.title: Is This What a Post-Literate Era Looks Like?
item.description: It’s not the children who stopped reading. It’s their grandparents.

item.title: Lionel Messi Is Walking Into History
item.description: Messi doesn’t walk because he’s left the game. He walks in order to enter it.

item.title: America Without Capitalism?
item.description: What would socialists do to the American economy? On “Interesting Times,” Bhaskar Sunkara, author of “The Socialist Manifesto,” thinks we can have free markets without capitalism.

item.title: Falling Birthrates and America’s Future
item.description: Sampling the outpouring of responses to “The Population Forecasts Aren’t Grim Enough,” an Opinion guest essay by Lyman Stone. Also: The Ferrari faithful.

item.title: A Test Isn’t Racist. Assumptions About Black Kids Can Be.
item.description: All students can be properly prepared to take this challenging admissions exam.

item.title: Make America Safe From Parasites Again
item.description: Cramping, vomiting and diarrhea. Is this what “Make America Healthy Again” looks like? The recent outbreak of Cyclospora, a parasite that causes a food-borne illness that’s ruining summers across the nation, has us asking: Were those cuts to public health institutions worth it? And what’s in this salad?

item.title: Trump’s Election Denial Has Never Been More Dangerous
item.description: Don’t fall for dramatic claims based on selective declassification.

item.title: ICE Is Out of Control. Here’s What to Do About It.
item.description: Congress must push back against our inhumane immigration tactics.

item.title: The Odyssey, by Donald Trump
item.description: The president lacks not only a coherent strategic concept but an elementary understanding of what strategy is.

item.title: England Lost in the World Cup. They Were Always Going to Lose.
item.description: The football team, like Britain itself, is burdened by the dead weight of impossible expectations.

item.title: A Vision of a ‘Society Without Capitalism’
item.description: Can democratic socialism redeem the left without destroying capitalism?

item.title: The Wild West of Ultraprocessed Food
item.description: Salt and sugar taste great, and Big Food knows that. While ultraprocessed foods are formulated to keep us coming back for more, the Times Opinion writer Jessica Grose argues that a lack of regulation in the food industry means consumers pay the cost, not corporations.

item.title: We’re About to Find Out Whether Republican Senators Can Still Say No
item.description: Republicans should have the decency, patriotism and self-respect to vote against Mr. Blanche.

item.title: A Vision of a ‘Society Without Capitalism’
item.description: Can democratic socialism redeem the left without destroying capitalism?

item.title: Goalkeepers Don’t Need Your Sympathy
item.description: How new soccer strategies changed the way goalkeepers play.

item.title: How Worried Should You Be About Cyclospora?
item.description: We didn’t need an outbreak of a nightmare stomach bug. But here we are.

item.title: If the Democrats Actually Want to Win, This Is What They Have to Do
item.description: It is these issues that serve to reinforce the perception that Democrats are willing to sacrifice the interests of the working and middle classes.

item.title: Are We Still Supposed to Take This Conservative Legal Theory Seriously?
item.description: Has the court learned nothing?

item.title: This Is a Forever War in the Making
item.description: The vague language of the U.S.-Iran cease-fire memo laid the foundation for the war’s resumption.

item.title: Is Dating a Colleague a Good Idea?
item.description: Readers weigh the pros and cons of dating a co-worker, in response to an Opinion guest essay. Also: Killings in Minnesota; safety on the road.

item.title: The Dangerous Appeal of Cruelty
item.description: When did politics get so cruel? On “The Opinions,” David French argues that we’ve started confusing cruelty with courage, rewarding politicians who break norms and mistake outrage for strength.

item.title: The Seduction of Lindsey Graham
item.description: He could see the obvious truth that Trump was a malign and corrosive force in American politics — that is, until Trump won the White House.

item.title: The Lo-Fi Way I Broke My Scrolling Habit
item.description: I tried many remedies to break my social media habit. A homemade hack worked best.

item.title: Is This the Fastest Opinion Shift in American Politics?
item.description: David Wallace-Wells and Robinson Meyer discuss whether the gathering coalition of Americans against data centers could be the next Occupy Wall Street movement or Tea Party.

item.title: ‘It’s Chaos’: What New York’s Suburbs Can Tell Us About the Mood of the Country
item.description: The midterms are looming. We asked people in New York City’s suburbs how they feel about the state of America.

item.title: How Is Your America Doing?
item.description: The midterms are looming. We asked people in New York City’s suburbs how they feel about the state of America.

item.title: The Ridiculous Rise of Victor Marx
item.description: Once started, the process of voter radicalization isn’t easy to modulate.

item.title: Lindsey Graham’s Choice
item.description: Would the world have been safer if Graham hadn’t cozied up to Trump?

item.title: Lindsey Graham Made Trumpism in His Own Image
item.description: He bent the knee and made the most of it.

item.title: The Democratic Establishment Had This Coming
item.description: The Democratic Party needs shaking up.

item.title: Some Positive (and Negative) Thinking About Therapy
item.description: Readers respond to “Not Everyone Should Be in Therapy,” an Opinion guest essay by Harvey Lieberman.

item.title: We’re Living in a Tick Nightmare. It’s Time to Go to War.
item.description: With ticks, we’ve barely begun to fight back. The longer we wait, the more territory they will claim across both our geographic and psychic maps.

item.title: The Ukraine-Russia War Reaches a New Phase
item.description: The ground war in Ukraine has stalemated and the crucial fight has shifted to the skies — missiles and interceptors.

item.title: What the Fight With Anthropic Reveals About Free Speech in America
item.description: We won’t get the A.I. regulation we deserve until we change how we think about the right to code.

item.title: I Teach at an Elite College and I Inflate Grades. Help Me.
item.description: Easy A’s are an easy out.

item.title: Are Fears of American Gerontocracy Fair?
item.description: Donald Trump. Mitch McConnell. American politics is once again grappling with the realities of aging leadership. On “The Opinions,” David Wallace-Wells argues that the United States has become a gerontocracy — and that as leaders stay in power longer, concerns about their health, transparency and ability to govern become harder to ignore.

item.title: The World Must Not Forget About the Genocide in Sudan
item.description: The longer the fighting drags on, the greater the chance that regional instability will spread.

item.title: The Tears, the Songs and the Memories
item.description: Readers respond to an Opinion guest essay by Roger Rosenblatt about why we cry. Also: Abortion prosecutions; Presidents Trump and Nixon.

item.title: “The Decline and Fall of the Roman Empire” Isn’t Saying What You Think It Is
item.description: In the same year as America’s founding, Edward Gibbon wrote “Decline and Fall.” 250 years later, it still holds lessons for us.

item.title: The Burdens and Benefits of Big Families
item.description: Readers respond to “Life Is Better With Siblings,” an Opinion guest essay by Catherine Ruth Pakaluk.

item.title: The World Is Cutting Ties With America. It’s Already Costing Us.
item.description: When other countries cut ties, Americans pay.

item.title: Serena Williams Is Back. Thank Goodness.
item.description: Until Serena Williams announced her return to tennis at 44, I never realized how much I needed her.

item.title: In Sudan, a Preventable Atrocity
item.description: Hundreds of thousands of people in Sudan are bracing for their city to be overrun by a militia notorious for committing atrocities.

item.title: More Than One Way to Fix Social Security
item.description: Readers respond to two guest essays about how to cover the shortfall in the program’s funding before it’s too late.

item.title: The Liberal American Jew’s Dilemma
item.description: Over the years, I have moved toward the way of being Jewish that my father rejected.

item.title: SpaceX, Elon Musk and the ‘Dumb Money’ Problem
item.description: Must what goes up also come down?

item.title: The Problem With Google’s A.I. Overview
item.description: What used to be a meandering journey is now an immediate arrival at your destination.

item.title: Does Matt Damon Look Greek? No. So Stop Complaining About Lupita Nyong’o.
item.description: Helen of Troy was a legendary beauty, but also so much more.

item.title: ‘There’s No Graham Platner Without Donald Trump’: 3 Writers on the Fiasco in Maine
item.description: How much have the rules of politics changed?

item.title: Did We Make the Wrong Bet on Big A.I.?
item.description: Maybe Anthropic and OpenAI are not the future of artificial intelligence.

item.title: JD Vance Is Worried
item.description: The vice president’s theology comes in book form.

item.title: Bring Back the SAT, California
item.description: Under the test-blind policy, more students are thrown into classes for which they are unprepared.

item.title: Israel Is Not Invincible
item.description: There is suddenly daylight between the Trump administration and Netanyahu.

item.title: Five Questions That Will Determine America’s Next 50 Years
item.description: The truth worth celebrating this summer is that our country is still being made.

item.title: What We Should Learn From Nordic Happiness
item.description: Scandinavia is the land of five-week vacations, virtually free university, one-year parental leaves, easy child care and very happy citizens.

item.title: Founding Father vs. Foundering Toddler
item.description: No. 1 puts No. 45/47 to shame.

item.title: This Pathetic Groveling Is No Way to Rebuild a Party
item.description: The Democrats’ constant solicitations for cash do not make their supporters feel like they are on a winning team.

item.title: The Transgender Sports Decision Was About Something Deeper Than Law
item.description: It’s never just a game.

item.title: Five Words That Shook the World
item.description: It wasn’t Thomas Jefferson who made “All men are created equal” actually mean something.
